evidence: HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling provides a consistent top-level error boundary: IAE → 400, IOException → 500, Exception → 500. All handler methods are wrapped through this.
GrammarConfigLoader throws IOException with descriptive messages for every config error path; wraps in IllegalStateException with actionable startup messages in createDefaultEnglish().
BlackLabQueryExecutor constructor catches bare Exception (not IOException or specific BlackLab exceptions) and wraps in IOException — overly broad catch loses the specific exception type.

CollocateQueryHelper accepts @Nullable BlackLabIndex at construction time (documented as test-only allowance) but defers null detection to individual method calls via Objects.requireNonNull — this means production misuse surfaces as a runtime NPE wrapped in IOException rather than at object construction.

SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles has explicit null check 'if (seedNouns == null) throw new IllegalArgumentException' on a @NonNull annotated parameter — redundant defensive check inconsistent with the rest of the codebase which relies on @NonNull annotations.

SketchHandlers.executeRelationQueries catches IOException per relation and adds to a relationErrors list, allowing the sketch to return partial results. This is an intentional 'continue on error' pattern that is not documented at the method level.

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti calls HttpApiUtils.sendError(exchange, 400, ...) directly for the seeds.size() < 2 check, bypassing the wrapWithErrorHandling pipeline used for all other 400-class validation in sibling methods.
BlackLabConllUIndexer constructor catches bare Exception and wraps to IOException — same broad catch pattern as BlackLabQueryExecutor.
LogDiceCalculator.compute throws IllegalArgumentException for non-positive inputs, which is appropriate for a pure computation utility.
CollocateQueryHelper.getTotalFrequency catches RuntimeException and wraps to IOException with a descriptive message — acceptable boundary translation.

strengths: HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling provides a clean, testable top-level error boundary shared by all handlers.
GrammarConfigLoader error messages are specific and actionable throughout all failure paths.
LogDiceCalculator has explicit precondition checks with clear exception messages.
Partial-result behavior in SketchHandlers (continue on relation error) is a reasonable design choice.
error_consistency.issue_character: Error handling strategy is inconsistent at integration points: broad Exception catches at constructor boundaries lose specificity, one validation path bypasses the uniform error pipeline, and a redundant null check on a @NonNull parameter signals mixed strategy between annotation-based and runtime-defensive approaches.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The codebase has a well-designed top-level error boundary and consistent IOException-wrapping at I/O boundaries. The score is held at 62 because of three compounding inconsistencies: the broad catch patterns at construction time, the direct sendError bypass of the error pipeline in multi-seed validation, and the redundant null check on @NonNull that signals unresolved strategy. Each issue is individually minor but together they reduce predictability of failure modes.

issues.summary: BlackLabQueryExecutor and BlackLabConllUIndexer catch bare Exception at construction, losing specific failure context
evidence: BlackLabQueryExecutor constructor: 'catch (Exception e) { throw new IOException("Failed to open index: " + e.getMessage(), e); }' — catches Exception broadly.
BlackLabConllUIndexer constructor: 'catch (Exception e) { throw new IOException("Failed to create index: " + e.getMessage(), e); }' — same pattern.
Callers cannot distinguish a permission error from a corrupt index from a configuration error based on the IOException type alone.
issues.suggestion: Catch the specific BlackLab exception types (e.g. InvalidQuery, or the checked exception from BlackLab.open()) and translate each to a descriptive IOException. Add an 'index open error type' note in the exception message (e.g. 'corrupt index', 'permission denied') to preserve failure context.

issues.summary: handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti calls sendError() directly for seeds.size() < 2, bypassing the uniform IAE → 400 pipeline
evidence: handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti line ~130: 'HttpApiUtils.sendError(exchange, 400, "Multi-seed exploration requires at least 2 seeds...")' — direct error send.
All other 400-class validation in the same class uses 'throw new IllegalArgumentException(...)' which wrapWithErrorHandling intercepts.
handleSemanticFieldComparison line ~178: 'throw new IllegalArgumentException("Comparison requires at least 2 seed nouns...")' — uses the uniform pattern for an equivalent check.
issues.suggestion: Replace the direct sendError call with 'throw new IllegalArgumentException("Multi-seed exploration requires at least 2 seeds; received " + seeds.size())' to match the pattern used everywhere else in the class and in handleSemanticFieldComparison.

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles performs an explicit null check on a @NonNull annotated parameter
evidence: compareCollocateProfiles signature: 'public @NonNull ComparisonResult compareCollocateProfiles(@NonNull Set<String> seedNouns, @NonNull ExplorationOptions opts)'
Method body line ~313: 'if (seedNouns == null) { throw new IllegalArgumentException("seedNouns must not be null"); }' — redundant check.
No other public method in the codebase performs explicit null checks on @NonNull parameters — this is a localized inconsistency.
issues.suggestion: Remove the explicit null check on seedNouns — the @NonNull annotation is the declared contract. If runtime enforcement is desired, use Objects.requireNonNull() consistently across all @NonNull parameters (or rely on annotation-processing null analysis tools).

root_causes: Construction of BlackLab objects can throw a variety of undocumented runtime exceptions; the broad catch was a pragmatic workaround rather than typed exception handling.
The multi-seed validation was added as a quick guard without aligning to the existing error pipeline pattern.
